Chapters 6 & 7 Exercises — Bayes Rules!

Worked solutions for the Lecture 4 suggested exercises: 6.2, 6.3, 6.8, 7.4, 7.9

Author

Johnny van Doorn

Published

June 12, 2026

These solutions accompany Lecture 4 (Obtaining the Posterior: Grid Approximation & MCMC). They cover the suggested exercises from Bayes Rules! Chapter 6 (approximating the posterior) and Chapter 7 (MCMC under the hood). The exercise prompts are quoted from the book; the worked solutions, code, and figures are our own.

Remember the exam scope: you do not need to implement samplers by hand or use Stan, but you should understand the intuition and be able to read and interpret the diagnostics shown below.

Chapter 6: Approximating the Posterior

Exercise 6.2 (Trace plot diagnostics)

For each MCMC simulation scenario described below, sketch by hand what a single chain trace plot might look like for each simulation.

  1. The chain is mixing too slowly.
  2. The chain has high correlation.
  3. The chain has a tendency to get “stuck.”
  4. The chain has no problems!

Solution. A trace plot shows the chain value \(\theta^{(t)}\) on the \(y\)-axis against the iteration \(t\) on the \(x\)-axis. Rather than sketch by hand, we simulate four chains that each exhibit one of these behaviours. The healthy chain (d) is the benchmark, the so-called “hairy caterpillar.”

Code 
set.seed(1)
N <- 500

# helper: simulate an AR(1) chain  x_t = phi * x_{t-1} + noise
ar1 <- function(N, phi, sd = 1) {
  x <- numeric(N)
  for (i in 2:N) x[i] <- phi * x[i - 1] + rnorm(1, 0, sd)
  x
}

# (a) mixing too slowly: extremely persistent -> long, slow waves that
#     have not explored the whole space within N iterations
slow <- ar1(N, phi = 0.995, sd = 0.25)

# (b) high correlation: stationary but very smooth, "snake-like"
hicorr <- ar1(N, phi = 0.9, sd = 0.5)

# (c) gets stuck: random-walk proposals accepted only ~12% of the time,
#     so the chain holds the same value for long stretches
stuck <- numeric(N)
for (i in 2:N) {
  prop <- stuck[i - 1] + rnorm(1, 0, 1.5)
  stuck[i] <- if (runif(1) < 0.12) prop else stuck[i - 1]
}

# (d) no problems: near-independent draws -> dense "hairy caterpillar"
healthy <- ar1(N, phi = 0.05, sd = 1)

par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(3.6, 3.6, 2.4, 1), mgp = c(2.1, 0.7, 0))
plot(slow,    type = "l", col = "#01426A", main = "(a) Mixing too slowly",
     xlab = "iteration", ylab = expression(theta))
plot(hicorr,  type = "l", col = "#01426A", main = "(b) High correlation",
     xlab = "iteration", ylab = expression(theta))
plot(stuck,   type = "l", col = "#01426A", main = "(c) Tendency to get stuck",
     xlab = "iteration", ylab = expression(theta))
plot(healthy, type = "l", col = "#01426A", main = "(d) No problems!",
     xlab = "iteration", ylab = expression(theta))
  • (a) Mixing too slowly: the trace drifts in long, slow waves and takes many iterations to wander from one part of the space to another. It looks like a meandering river rather than rapid back-and-forth.
  • (b) High correlation: consecutive values are nearly identical, giving a smooth, “snake-like” line. Neighbouring draws carry almost no new information.
  • (c) Gets stuck: the trace shows flat horizontal plateaus where the chain rejected many proposals and stayed put, interrupted by occasional jumps.
  • (d) No problems! A dense, fuzzy band oscillating rapidly around a stable level, with no trends and no flat spots: the “hairy caterpillar” we hope to see.

Exercise 6.3 (MCMC woes)

For each MCMC simulation scenario described below, describe how the scenario could impact the posterior approximation.

  1. The chain is mixing too slowly.
  2. The chain has high correlation.
  3. The chain has a tendency to get “stuck.”

Solution.

(a) Mixing too slowly. Within a fixed number of iterations the chain has not visited the whole posterior, so the approximation is incomplete and biased: regions the chain has not reached yet are under-represented. Posterior summaries (mean, credible intervals, tail probabilities) computed from such a chain are unreliable, and we would need a far longer run (or a better sampler) before trusting them.

(b) High correlation. Strongly autocorrelated draws contain a lot of redundant information, so the effective sample size is much smaller than the nominal number of iterations \(N\). The Monte Carlo error on any posterior estimate is therefore larger than \(N\) would suggest. Our summaries are noisier and less precise. Running the chain longer (or thinning) is needed to recover precision.

(c) Gets stuck. A chain that lingers too long at particular values over-represents that region and under-explores the rest of the posterior. If it gets stuck at an unrepresentative value, the approximation is biased and posterior summaries are skewed toward wherever the chain stalled. Sticking is often a symptom of a poorly tuned proposal (steps too large) or of multimodality.

In all three cases the underlying issue is the same: the simulated draws are not yet a representative sample from the posterior, so estimates based on them cannot be trusted until the diagnostics look healthy.

Exercise 6.8 (The Curse of Dimensionality)

As we note in this chapter, grid approximation suffers from the curse of dimensionality.

  1. Describe a situation in which we would want to have inference for multiple parameters (i.e., high-dimensional Bayesian models).
  2. In your own words, explain how dimensionality can affect grid approximation and why this is a curse.

Solution.

(a) A multi-parameter situation. Almost any realistic model has more than one unknown. For example, in a Normal model where neither the mean \(\mu\) nor the standard deviation \(\sigma\) is known, we already need joint inference for two parameters \((\mu, \sigma)\). A regression of, say, exam score on hours studied and test anxiety has four unknowns: an intercept, two slopes, and the residual standard deviation \((\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2, \sigma)\). Hierarchical models (e.g., a separate ability parameter for each of many participants) can have dozens or hundreds of parameters. In all of these we want the joint posterior over every parameter at once.

(b) Why dimensionality is a curse. Grid approximation evaluates the (unnormalised) posterior at every point of a grid that spans each parameter’s range. If we use \(K\) grid points per parameter and have \(p\) parameters, the grid contains \(K^p\) points; the cost grows exponentially in the number of parameters. Even with a modest \(K = 100\):

Code 
K <- 100
data.frame(
  parameters = c(1, 2, 3, 5, 10),
  grid_points = sprintf("%.0e", K^c(1, 2, 3, 5, 10))
)
parameters grid_points
1 1e+02
2 1e+04
3 1e+06
5 1e+10
10 1e+20

With ten parameters we already need \(100^{10} = 10^{20}\) evaluations, utterly infeasible. Worse, in high dimensions almost all of those grid points fall in regions of negligible posterior density, so the effort is largely wasted. This exponential blow-up is the “curse,” and it is exactly why we turn to MCMC, whose cost scales far more gently with dimension because it spends its time where the posterior mass actually is.

Chapter 7: MCMC Under the Hood

Exercise 7.4 (Tuning the Metropolis-Hastings)

In this exercise you will consider how to tune a Uniform proposal model with half-width \(w\) for a Metropolis-Hastings algorithm.

  1. Draw a trace plot for a tour where the Uniform proposal model uses a very small \(w\).
  2. Why is it problematic if \(w\) is too small, and hence defines the neighborhood around the current chain value too narrowly?
  3. Draw a trace plot for a tour where the Uniform proposal model uses a very large \(w\).
  4. Why is it problematic if \(w\) is too large, and hence defines the neighborhood too widely?
  5. Draw a trace plot for a tour where the Uniform proposal model uses a \(w\) that is neither too small or too large.
  6. Describe how you would go about finding an appropriate half-width \(w\) for a Uniform proposal model.

Solution. We use the book’s own one_mh_iteration() and mh_tour() functions (Section 7.3), which target the Normal–Normal posterior with prior \(\mu \sim N(0,1)\) and a single observation \(y = 6.25\) with likelihood SD \(0.75\) (so the posterior concentrates around \(\mu \approx 4\)). We run three 1000-step tours: a too-small \(w\), a too-large \(w\), and a well-tuned \(w\).

Code 
one_mh_iteration <- function(w, current) {
  # STEP 1: propose the next location from Unif(current - w, current + w)
  proposal <- runif(1, min = current - w, max = current + w)
  # STEP 2: decide whether to move there
  proposal_plaus <- dnorm(proposal, 0, 1) * dnorm(6.25, proposal, 0.75)
  current_plaus  <- dnorm(current, 0, 1) * dnorm(6.25, current, 0.75)
  alpha <- min(1, proposal_plaus / current_plaus)
  next_stop <- sample(c(proposal, current), size = 1, prob = c(alpha, 1 - alpha))
  data.frame(proposal, alpha, next_stop)
}

mh_tour <- function(N, w) {
  current <- 3
  mu <- rep(0, N)
  for (i in 1:N) {
    sim <- one_mh_iteration(w = w, current = current)
    mu[i] <- sim$next_stop
    current <- sim$next_stop
  }
  data.frame(iteration = 1:N, mu)
}

set.seed(84735)
tour_small <- mh_tour(N = 1000, w = 0.01)   # (a) very small w
tour_big   <- mh_tour(N = 1000, w = 100)    # (c) very large w
tour_good  <- mh_tour(N = 1000, w = 1)      # (e) well-tuned w
Code 
par(mfrow = c(3, 1), mar = c(3.6, 3.8, 2.4, 1), mgp = c(2.2, 0.7, 0))
plot(tour_small, type = "l", col = "#01426A",
     main = "(a) w = 0.01: too small", xlab = "iteration", ylab = expression(mu))
plot(tour_big, type = "l", col = "#01426A",
     main = "(c) w = 100: too large", xlab = "iteration", ylab = expression(mu))
plot(tour_good, type = "l", col = "#01426A",
     main = "(e) w = 1: well tuned", xlab = "iteration", ylab = expression(mu))

(b) Why too small \(w\) is problematic. When \(w\) is tiny the proposed value is almost identical to the current one, so it is nearly always accepted, but each step is minuscule. The chain creeps: it explores the posterior extremely slowly, produces highly correlated draws, and may not even reach the bulk of the posterior (around \(\mu \approx 4\)) within the run. High acceptance, but terrible mixing and a tiny effective sample size.

(d) Why too large \(w\) is problematic. When \(w\) is huge, most proposals land far out in the tails where the posterior plausibility is minute, so the acceptance probability \(\alpha\) is almost zero and the proposals are rejected. The chain therefore stays put for long stretches. The trace shows long flat plateaus. Again mixing is poor and the effective sample size is small, just for the opposite reason.

(e) A well-tuned \(w\) strikes a balance: proposals are far enough to make real progress but close enough to be accepted reasonably often. The trace becomes a healthy “hairy caterpillar” exploring the posterior around \(\mu \approx 4\).

(f) How to find an appropriate \(w\). Tune it empirically: run short pilot tours over a range of \(w\) values and inspect (i) the acceptance rate, a common rule of thumb for random-walk Metropolis is to aim for roughly 0.2–0.5, avoiding both ~1 (steps too small) and ~0 (steps too large); (ii) the trace plots, look for the hairy caterpillar rather than a slow snake or flat plateaus; and (iii) the effective sample size / autocorrelation, choosing the \(w\) that maximises effective samples per iteration. Exercise 7.9 makes the mechanism concrete by showing how \(\alpha\) changes with \(w\) for a single step.

Exercise 7.9 (One iteration with a Uniform proposal model)

The function one_mh_iteration() from the text utilizes a Uniform proposal model, \(\mu' \mid \mu \sim \text{Unif}(\mu - w, \mu + w)\) with half-width \(w = 1\). Starting from a current value of \(\mu = 3\) and using set.seed(1), run the code below and comment on the returned proposal, alpha, and next_stop values.

  1. one_mh_iteration(w = 0.01, current = 3)
  2. one_mh_iteration(w = 0.5, current = 3)
  3. one_mh_iteration(w = 1, current = 3)
  4. one_mh_iteration(w = 3, current = 3)

Solution. Using set.seed(1) before each call, the first runif() draw is the same (0.2655), so each proposal equals \(3 - w + 2w(0.2655) = 3 - 0.469\,w\), i.e. a step of the same shape scaled by \(w\). Larger \(w\) therefore lands further below the current value of 3 and further from the posterior’s high-density region near \(\mu \approx 4\), which is why \(\alpha\) collapses as \(w\) grows.

Code 
for (w in c(0.01, 0.5, 1, 3)) {
  set.seed(1)
  cat(sprintf("one_mh_iteration(w = %s, current = 3):\n", w))
  print(round(one_mh_iteration(w = w, current = 3), 5))
  cat("\n")
}
one_mh_iteration(w = 0.01, current = 3):
  proposal   alpha next_stop
1  2.99531 0.98703   2.99531

one_mh_iteration(w = 0.5, current = 3):
  proposal alpha next_stop
1  2.76551 0.483         3

one_mh_iteration(w = 1, current = 3):
  proposal   alpha next_stop
1  2.53102 0.20025         3

one_mh_iteration(w = 3, current = 3):
  proposal   alpha next_stop
1  1.59305 0.00128         3

Comments.

  • (a) \(w = 0.01\): proposal \(\approx 2.995\), essentially on top of the current value. Its plausibility is almost identical, so \(\alpha \approx 0.99\) and the proposal is accepted (next_stop \(\approx 2.995\)). Tiny, near-certain steps, the “too small \(w\)” behaviour of Exercise 7.4(b).
  • (b) \(w = 0.5\): proposal \(\approx 2.766\), a bit further from the posterior mode, giving \(\alpha \approx 0.48\). Here the coin flip lands on reject, so next_stop \(= 3\).
  • (c) \(w = 1\): proposal \(\approx 2.531\), further still, so \(\alpha \approx 0.20\) and the move is again rejected (next_stop \(= 3\)).
  • (d) \(w = 3\): proposal \(\approx 1.593\), far out in the low-density tail, so \(\alpha \approx 0.001\) and the proposal is almost surely rejected (next_stop \(= 3\)).

The trend is exactly the tuning story: as \(w\) grows the proposals reach further from the current location, but because they move away from the bulk of this posterior the acceptance probability \(\alpha\) falls steeply, from \(\approx 0.99\) down to \(\approx 0.001\). Too-small \(w\) accepts everything but barely moves; too-large \(w\) proposes bold jumps that are nearly always rejected. A well-chosen \(w\) lives in between.

Source: Johnson, A. A., Ott, M. Q., & Dogucu, M. (2022). Bayes Rules! An Introduction to Applied Bayesian Modeling. Chapman & Hall/CRC. Exercise prompts quoted from Chapters 6–7; one_mh_iteration() and mh_tour() reproduced from Section 7.3.
